text: Transrectal natural orifice specimen extraction, a safe and feasible technique in patients undergoing subtotal colectomy.

Traditional laparoscopic subtotal colectomy requires a mini-laparotomy or larger than usual ileostomy incision for specimen extraction, which contributes to increased post-operative pain and long-term risk of hernia formation.
Transrectal specimen extraction has the potential to reduce this morbidity and improve surgical outcomes.
We have performed TR nose in several patients during the last year.
This video aims to demonstrate the six steps for TR nose as developed in our institution.

The surgeon completes laparoscopic subtotal colonic mobilization, a standard procedure familiar to most laparoscopic colorectal surgeons, involving the careful manipulation and positioning of the colon using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon performs specimen transection at both proximal and distal ends, preparing the tissue for extraction.
The surgeon performs a rectal washout, flushing the rectal cavity with a sterile solution to cleanse the area before proceeding with further steps.
The surgeon inserts washout tubing into the apex of the rectal stump to cleanse the stump thoroughly and minimize contamination of the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon opens the rectal stump, preparing it for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon extracts the specimen from the abdominal cavity, completing the laparoscopic procedure.
We've tried several methods and our favoured approach is to insert a nylon tape via the perineum into the pelvis.
The surgeon pulls a protective plastic sheath up into the pelvis, following the insertion of washout tubing into the rectal stump.
The protective plastic sheath provides a smooth surface, facilitating the specimen's descent and extraction through the rectum.
The specimen is inserted into the opened rectal stump, with extraction facilitated by pushing from above and pulling from below by a second operator.
The majority of the specimen is visibly extracted, but gaseous acetyl distention is observed, complicating the extraction process.
The surgeon makes an incision in the partially extracted specimen located high up near the anal canal, preparing for further manipulation or extraction.
The surgeon inserts a flatus tube into the specimen, which is positioned within the rectal stump and protected by a plastic sheath.
The deflated specimen is fully extracted from the rectum, completing the removal process.
The surgeon cross-staples the rectal stump and performs an ileostomy, completing the procedure.
A more challenging case is shown now.
The distal specimen is successfully passed out, but the extraction of the bulky transverse colon presents difficulty.
The surgeon makes several incisions into the colon and performs sequential decompressions using a flatus tube, visibly reducing the size of the colon for easier handling.
The surgeon identifies a bulky omentum as an obstacle, hindering the progress of the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the omentum away from the specimen, carefully separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon resumes the extraction process, maneuvering around the bulky transverse colon to facilitate the removal of the specimen.
The surgeon addresses gaseous distention in the cecum, facilitating the successful extraction of the specimen.
In conclusion, a stepwise approach to transrectal specimen extraction is feasible and it may be applied to patients having a laparoscopic subtotal colectomy.
Thank you for watching this video.
